questions.question: The vaccine against terrible cattle disease rind pest was made by __________.
questions.explanation: Prof. Tilahun Yilma developed the vaccine against rinderpest (cattle plague), a devastating viral disease of cattle. His work at the USDA's Plum Island Animal Disease Center led to the creation of a recombinant vaccine that was crucial in the global eradication of rinderpest in 2011 — the first animal disease to be eradicated worldwide.

questions.question: Which of the following Ethiopian Biologist has been honored by developing high yielding strains of sorghum?
questions.explanation: Dr. Gebissa Ejetta is an Ethiopian plant breeder and geneticist renowned for developing high-yielding and drought-resistant strains of sorghum. His work has significantly contributed to food security in Africa, and he received the World Food Prize in 2009 for his contributions to improving sorghum production for millions of people in sub-Saharan Africa.

questions.question: Ethiopian Biologist who worked on effects of chemical pesticides on human is ___________.
questions.explanation: Prof. Yealemtsehay Mekonnen is known for her research on the effects of chemical pesticides on human health, particularly in the Ethiopian context. Her work has contributed to understanding the health risks associated with pesticide exposure and has informed policies on pesticide regulation and safety in Ethiopia.

questions.question: Ethiopian Biologist who has made a soap berry to eradicate snail which is intermediate host of bilharzia:
questions.explanation: Dr. Aklilu Lemma discovered that the endod plant (soap berry, Phytolacca dodecandra) could be used to eradicate freshwater snails that serve as intermediate hosts for bilharzia (schistosomiasis). His pioneering work provided an affordable and environmentally friendly method of controlling schistosomiasis, a disease that affects millions of people in tropical regions.

questions.question: Among the following renowned Ethiopian Biologists who discover two 160,000 years old human skulls which have a big impact on the study of human evolution?
questions.explanation: Dr. Berhane Asfaw co-discovered the Herto fossils — two 160,000-year-old Homo sapiens idaltu skulls in the Middle Awash region of Ethiopia in 1997. These fossils are among the oldest anatomically modern human remains ever found and provided crucial evidence for the 'Out of Africa' theory of human evolution, significantly impacting our understanding of human origins.

questions.question: All are the major research areas of EHNRI except:
options: Nutritional state of mothers and baby
questions.explanation: EHNRI (Ethiopian Health and Nutrition Research Institute) focuses on health and nutrition research areas including HIV vaccine trials, tuberculosis research, and nutritional studies of mothers and babies. Forestry is not a research area of EHNRI; it falls under the mandate of other institutions like the Ethiopian Environment and Forest Research Institute or the Ministry of Agriculture.

questions.question: Ethiopian Biological research center which is involved in mechanized farming is:
questions.explanation: EIAR (Ethiopian Institute of Agricultural Research) is the primary research institution involved in mechanized farming in Ethiopia. It conducts research on crop improvement, agricultural mechanization, irrigation, and sustainable farming practices. The other institutions focus on different areas: ALIPB on animal health, AHRI on health/leprosy research, and IBC on biodiversity conservation.

questions.question: Ethiopian Biological research center which is highly devoted on research related to Leprosy is:
questions.explanation: AHRI (Armauer Hansen Research Institute) is highly devoted to research related to leprosy. Named after Gerhard Henrik Armauer Hansen who discovered the leprosy bacterium, AHRI was established in Addis Ababa near the All Africa Leprosy and Rehabilitation Training Center (ALERT). It conducts extensive research on leprosy, tuberculosis, and other infectious diseases.

questions.question: Among the following institutes which one is working on the management of the ecosystem?
questions.explanation: IBC (Institute of Biodiversity Conservation) works on the management and conservation of ecosystems in Ethiopia. Its mandate includes preserving the country's biodiversity, managing ecosystems, and ensuring sustainable use of biological resources. The other institutes focus on different areas: ALIPB on animal health, EHNRI on health and nutrition, and EIAR on agricultural research.

questions.question: The resolution power of a microscope used to:
questions.explanation: Resolution power (resolving power) refers to the ability of a microscope to distinguish between two closely spaced objects as separate entities — essentially, to show objects clearly with fine detail. It is different from magnification (which increases apparent size) and brightness. Higher resolution means better clarity and the ability to see finer details of the specimen.

questions.question: Preparation of specimen to see under the microscope is known as ____________.
questions.explanation: Mounting is the process of preparing a specimen on a microscope slide for observation. This involves placing the specimen on a slide, adding a mounting medium, and covering it with a coverslip. While staining is a technique used to add color to specimens for better visibility, mounting refers to the overall preparation process of making a specimen ready for microscopic examination.

questions.question: Cells that are adapted to carry out a particular body function are:
questions.explanation: Specialized cells (differentiated cells) are cells that have undergone differentiation to develop specific structures and functions suited to particular roles in the body. Examples include neurons (for nerve impulse transmission), muscle cells (for contraction), and red blood cells (for oxygen transport). This specialization occurs through differential gene expression during development.

questions.question: A cell with a large number of mitochondria is expected to:
questions.explanation: Mitochondria are the powerhouses of the cell, responsible for producing ATP through aerobic respiration. A cell with a large number of mitochondria has a high energy demand and is expected to produce large amounts of ATP. Examples include muscle cells, liver cells, and kidney cells, which all require significant energy to perform their functions.

questions.question: Which of the following structures of the cell are involved in the synthesis of protein?
questions.explanation: Ribosomes are the cellular structures directly responsible for protein synthesis (translation). They read the mRNA sequence and assemble amino acids into polypeptide chains according to the genetic code. Ribosomes can be free in the cytoplasm or attached to the rough endoplasmic reticulum. The Golgi body modifies and packages proteins after synthesis, but does not synthesize them.

questions.question: Which of the following organelle is the site of photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts are the organelles where photosynthesis occurs. They contain the green pigment chlorophyll, which captures light energy, and have an internal structure of thylakoid membranes where the light-dependent reactions take place. The stroma contains enzymes for the Calvin cycle. Leucoplasts are colorless plastids that store starch, not photosynthetic sites.

questions.question: The movement of water molecules from a region of high concentration to low concentration is:
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the movement of water molecules from a region of higher water concentration (lower solute concentration) to a region of lower water concentration (higher solute concentration) through a selectively permeable membrane. While diffusion refers to the movement of any substance from high to low concentration, osmosis specifically refers to the movement of water across a semi-permeable membrane.

questions.question: Which of the following is true about rate of diffusion?
options: Diffusion increases when the temperature decreases
Diffusion increases when the temperature increases
Diffusion decreases when there is very high concentration difference between two areas
Diffusion increases when there is very low concentration difference between two areas
questions.explanation: Diffusion rate increases with temperature because higher temperatures provide more kinetic energy to molecules, causing them to move faster and spread more quickly. Additionally, diffusion increases with greater concentration difference (gradient), not decreases. These relationships follow Fick's law of diffusion, where the rate is proportional to temperature and concentration gradient.

questions.question: The movement of substances from the region of higher concentration to lower concentration along concentration gradient is called ________.
questions.explanation: Passive transport is the general term for the movement of substances along their concentration gradient (from high to low concentration) without requiring energy input (ATP). This includes diffusion, osmosis, and facilitated diffusion. Active transport moves substances against the concentration gradient and requires energy. While diffusion is a type of passive transport, the question asks for the general category.

questions.question: If an animal cell is put in concentrated salt solution which of the following event will happen?
questions.explanation: An animal cell placed in a concentrated salt solution (hypertonic solution) will lose water by osmosis because the external solution has a lower water concentration than the cell's interior. As water moves out of the cell, the cell shrinks (crenates). Plasmolysis specifically refers to plant cells where the cell membrane pulls away from the cell wall; animal cells without a cell wall simply shrink.

questions.question: The most common monomer of carbohydrate is a molecule of:
questions.explanation: Glucose is the most common monomer (building block) of carbohydrates. It is a monosaccharide (simple sugar) with the formula C6H12O6 and serves as the primary building block for larger carbohydrates like starch, cellulose, and glycogen. Phospholipids and fatty acids are components of lipids, not carbohydrates, and triglycerides are types of lipids.

questions.question: When 50 amino acids joined together through condensation reaction, how many peptide bonds are formed and water molecules are released respectively?
questions.explanation: When amino acids join through condensation reactions, each peptide bond forms between the carboxyl group of one amino acid and the amino group of the next, releasing one water molecule per bond. With 50 amino acids, there are 49 peptide bonds (one less than the number of amino acids) and 49 water molecules released. The formula is: peptide bonds = n - 1, where n is the number of amino acids.

questions.question: Unsaturated fatty acids are differ from saturated fatty acids except:
options: They have one or more double bonds
They are more common in plants
They are liquids at room temperature
questions.explanation: The question asks which statement does NOT describe unsaturated fatty acids. Unsaturated fatty acids have one or more double bonds (option B), are more common in plants (option C), and are liquids at room temperature (option D). However, having all single bonds (option A) describes saturated fatty acids, not unsaturated ones. Therefore, this is the exception — it is NOT a characteristic of unsaturated fatty acids.

questions.question: Which of the following minerals is important constituent of bone and teeth needed for many metabolic processes?
questions.explanation: Calcium is a crucial mineral that is a primary constituent of bones and teeth (in the form of calcium phosphate/hydroxyapatite). Beyond its structural role, calcium is essential for many metabolic processes including muscle contraction, nerve impulse transmission, blood clotting, and enzyme activation. While phosphorus is also in bones, calcium is the mineral most commonly emphasized for bone and teeth composition.

questions.question: One of the following vitamins is needed for the formation of connective tissues which holds the body together?
questions.explanation: Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) is essential for the synthesis of collagen, which is the main structural protein in connective tissues throughout the body. Collagen provides strength and structure to skin, bones, tendons, ligaments, and blood vessels. Vitamin C deficiency causes scurvy, characterized by weakened connective tissue, bleeding gums, and poor wound healing.

questions.question: Water is crucial in human body for a number of reasons. Which one is not correct about their functions?
options: It is a universal solvent in any environment
It is involved in the transport of substance around the body
It is involved in temperature regulation by loss of heat through sweating
It is not involved in any chemical reaction which take place in the body
questions.explanation: Water is involved in numerous chemical reactions in the body, including hydrolysis reactions (breaking down molecules), condensation reactions (building molecules), and as a reactant or product in metabolic processes like photosynthesis and cellular respiration. The statement that water is not involved in any chemical reaction is incorrect, making it the right answer to the question.

questions.question: Which one of the following is not the part of a balanced diet?
questions.explanation: Cellulose is not considered part of a balanced diet because humans lack the enzyme cellulase needed to digest it. While cellulose (dietary fiber) is important for digestive health, it does not provide nutritional value as a food component. Proteins, lipids, and carbohydrates are the three main macronutrients that are essential parts of a balanced diet, providing energy and building blocks for the body.

questions.question: Which one of the following is orders of digestive system that correctly illustrates from the beginning to the end?
questions.explanation: The correct order of the digestive process is: ingestion (taking food in), digestion (breaking food down mechanically and chemically), absorption (taking digested nutrients into the bloodstream), assimilation (using absorbed nutrients by body cells), and egestion (eliminating undigested waste). This sequential process ensures nutrients are properly extracted and utilized by the body.

questions.question: In grade 9 laboratory class, the teacher gives the students a sample of food and asked them to investigate the presence of glucose. What kind of test and result will enable the students to confirm the presence of glucose respectively?
options: Emulsion test and milky white colour
Iodine test and blue black colour
questions.explanation: Benedict's test is used to detect the presence of reducing sugars like glucose. When Benedict's reagent is heated with a sample containing glucose, it produces a color change from blue to green, yellow, orange, or red (depending on concentration), with orange indicating a moderate to high concentration. Biuret test detects proteins (purple), emulsion test detects lipids (milky white), and iodine test detects starch (blue-black).

questions.question: Which of the following factor affecting enzyme activity without denaturing the enzyme molecule?
questions.explanation: Low temperature reduces enzyme activity without denaturing the enzyme molecule. At low temperatures, molecules have less kinetic energy, resulting in fewer collisions between enzyme and substrate, but the enzyme's three-dimensional structure remains intact. When temperature returns to optimal, the enzyme resumes normal activity. In contrast, high temperature and extreme pH denature (permanently alter) the enzyme's structure.

questions.question: The shapes of different teeth in humans are ideally suited to their various functions. Which type of teeth is not correctly matched with their functions?
questions.explanation: Canines are pointed teeth adapted for tearing and ripping food, not for chewing. Chewing is the function of premolars and molars. Premolars have flat surfaces for chewing and crushing, molars have broad surfaces for grinding and crushing, and incisors have sharp edges for biting and cutting. The incorrect match is canines being paired with chewing.

questions.question: In a Benedict's test for sucrose, why does the test sample first boil with hydrochloric acid?
questions.explanation: Sucrose is a non-reducing sugar that does not react directly with Benedict's reagent. Boiling with hydrochloric acid hydrolyzes sucrose into its component monosaccharides — glucose and fructose — which are both reducing sugars. After hydrolysis, these reducing sugars can then be detected by Benedict's test. This two-step process is necessary because Benedict's test only detects reducing sugars.

questions.question: Which deficient nutrients are correctly paired with their deficiency diseases?
questions.explanation: Vitamin A deficiency causes night blindness (nyctalopia), as vitamin A is essential for the synthesis of rhodopsin, the visual pigment needed for vision in low light. The other pairings are incorrect: scurvy is caused by vitamin C deficiency (not protein), marasmus is caused by overall calorie/protein deficiency (not specifically iron), and beri-beri is caused by vitamin B1 (thiamine) deficiency (not carbohydrate).

questions.question: When you breathe out during gas exchange, all of the following happens to the breathing movement except?
options: The volume of the thorax decrease
questions.explanation: During exhalation (breathing out), the diaphragm moves upward and the intercostal muscles relax, causing the thorax volume to decrease. This decrease in volume increases the pressure inside the chest cavity (Boyle's law), forcing air out of the lungs. The statement that pressure decreases (option B) is incorrect — pressure actually increases during exhalation, making it the exception.

questions.question: The muscle that separate the chest cavity from the abdominal cavity is known as:
questions.explanation: The diaphragm is a dome-shaped muscular sheet that separates the thoracic (chest) cavity from the abdominal cavity. It plays a crucial role in breathing — when it contracts, it flattens and increases thoracic volume for inhalation, and when it relaxes, it returns to its dome shape for exhalation. Trachoma is an eye disease, and the epiglottis prevents food from entering the trachea.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct pathway of breathing from the outer to the inner parts?
options: Nasal cavity → trachea → bronchus → bronchioles → alveolus
Alveolus → bronchus → bronchioles → trachea → nasal cavity
Alveolus → bronchioles → bronchus → trachea → nasal cavity
Nasal cavity → alveolus → bronchus → trachea → bronchioles
questions.explanation: The correct pathway of air during inhalation from outside to the gas exchange surface is: nasal cavity → trachea → bronchus → bronchioles → alveolus. Air enters through the nose, passes down the trachea, into a bronchus, through progressively smaller bronchioles, and finally reaches the alveoli where gas exchange occurs. The other options either reverse the direction or mix up the order.

questions.question: While oxygen is carried by the red blood cells, most digested food and wastes are carried by the:
questions.explanation: Blood plasma is the liquid component of blood that carries most digested food (glucose, amino acids, fatty acids), waste products (urea, carbon dioxide as bicarbonate), hormones, and other dissolved substances. Red blood cells specifically carry oxygen bound to hemoglobin, white blood cells fight infection, and platelets are involved in blood clotting.

questions.question: The absolute maximum amount of air you can take into or breathe out of your lungs is called:
questions.explanation: Vital capacity is the maximum amount of air that can be expelled from the lungs after taking the deepest possible breath. It equals the sum of tidal volume, inspiratory reserve volume, and expiratory reserve volume. Tidal volume is the normal amount of air breathed in/out during regular breathing, and residual volume is the air that always remains in the lungs and cannot be voluntarily expelled.

questions.question: Which of the following products are formed during anaerobic respiration of one glucose molecule in animal cells?
questions.explanation: In animal cells, anaerobic respiration (lactic acid fermentation) breaks down glucose into lactic acid and produces a net gain of 2 ATP. The process regenerates NAD+ from NADH, allowing glycolysis to continue. While CO2 is not technically produced in lactic acid fermentation (only in alcoholic fermentation), option B is the intended answer in the Ethiopian curriculum context. Option A describes alcoholic fermentation in yeast.

questions.question: Which of the following is the type of blood vessel that carries deoxygenated blood from the heart to lungs?
questions.explanation: The pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle of the heart to the lungs for oxygenation. It is the only artery in the body that carries deoxygenated blood. The pulmonary vein carries oxygenated blood from the lungs back to the heart. Systematic/systemic circulation refers to the circuit from the heart to the body and back, not a specific vessel.

questions.question: The right side chamber valve which directs the flow of blood by opening and closing is called:
questions.explanation: The tricuspid valve is located between the right atrium and right ventricle. It has three flaps (cusps) that open to allow blood to flow from the right atrium to the right ventricle and close to prevent backflow. The bicuspid (mitral) valve is on the left side of the heart, and the vena cava is a large vein, not a valve.

questions.question: Which one of the following statement is true about pulmonary circulation?
options: Blood flows from the heart to all around the body and back again
Carry only oxygenated blood from the body
Blood flows from the heart to lungs and back again
It transport oxygenated blood from the heart and back again
questions.explanation: Pulmonary circulation is the pathway of blood from the heart to the lungs and back again. Deoxygenated blood is pumped from the right ventricle through the pulmonary artery to the lungs, where it picks up oxygen and releases CO2, then returns via the pulmonary veins to the left atrium. Option A describes systemic circulation, and options B and D are incorrect descriptions of pulmonary circulation.

questions.question: Which organic molecule in our body has functions as antigens and antibodies of immune system?
questions.explanation: Proteins function as both antigens and antibodies in the immune system. Antibodies (immunoglobulins) are protein molecules produced by B-lymphocytes that recognize and bind to specific antigens. Antigens are typically protein molecules on the surface of pathogens that trigger an immune response. Both are protein-based, making proteins the correct organic molecule category.

questions.question: The blood group of human being which is commonly referred to as 'universal acceptor' is:
questions.explanation: Blood group AB is known as the 'universal acceptor' (or universal recipient) because people with this blood type have both A and B antigens on their red blood cells and no anti-A or anti-B antibodies in their plasma. This means they can receive blood from any blood group (A, B, AB, or O) without an immune reaction. Blood group O is the 'universal donor.'

questions.question: All of the following factors that can increase the risks of developing hypertension except?
questions.explanation: Reducing salt intake actually decreases the risk of developing hypertension, so it is the exception. High salt intake is a major risk factor for hypertension because excess sodium causes water retention, increasing blood volume and pressure. The other factors — excessive alcohol consumption, increasing age, and sedentary lifestyle — all increase the risk of developing hypertension.

questions.question: The modern technological instruments are learnt from nature by the process of:
questions.explanation: Modern technological instruments are learned from nature through the process of imitating (biomimicry). Biomimicry involves studying nature's designs and processes and then imitating them to solve human problems. Examples include imitating bird flight for airplane design, imitating lotus leaf surface for self-cleaning materials, and imitating gecko feet for adhesives. This biological inspiration through imitation drives innovation.

questions.question: The relevance and promise of biology as a field of science can be best explained by its application:
questions.explanation: The relevance and promise of biology are best demonstrated through its applications in agriculture and genetic engineering. Biology has revolutionized crop production through genetic modification, improved livestock breeding, pest control, and development of drought-resistant varieties. Genetic engineering enables the creation of GMOs, gene therapy, and biopharmaceuticals, showing biology's direct impact on human welfare and food security.

questions.question: Among the following which one is incorrect statement about the importance of nature for imitating technological products?
options: It is the sources of materials that enable to imitating
Used to develop modern technologies that provide services
Used to develop knowledge of problem solving ability
To provide limited resources and techniques for society
questions.explanation: Nature provides abundant, not limited, resources and techniques for society. The statement that nature provides 'limited resources and techniques' is incorrect and contradicts the vast potential of biomimicry. Nature offers countless designs, materials, and processes that can be studied and imitated. The other options correctly describe nature's importance as a source of materials, inspiration for modern technologies, and a basis for developing problem-solving skills.

questions.question: Which technological products have been made through imitation of termite mound architecture?
questions.explanation: Termite mound architecture has inspired innovations in building technology, particularly in natural ventilation systems. The Eastgate Centre in Harare, Zimbabwe, is a famous example — its ventilation system was designed based on the cooling chimney structure of termite mounds, reducing energy consumption by 90% compared to conventional buildings. Termites maintain constant temperature in their mounds through sophisticated air circulation designs.

questions.question: Which one of the following are the impact of biology on society and natural world except?
options: Ensuring food security by increasing productivity
Lack of involvement in application of microbial action
Production of antibiotics and treatment for bacterial infection
Achieving better supply of energy and clean water
questions.explanation: Biology is heavily involved in the application of microbial action — from fermentation for food production, to bioremediation for cleaning pollution, to the use of microbes in producing antibiotics, vitamins, and enzymes. The claim of 'lack of involvement in application of microbial action' is incorrect, making it the exception. Biology actively uses and applies microbial processes in numerous beneficial ways.

questions.question: Among which explanation is correctly concerned about ethical issue in biology?
options: It refers about rightness or wrongness use of biological discoveries
Concerned about only ethical treatment of plant species
Encourage issues about wrongly uses of technology against human
Focus on encouraging unethical treatment of research animals
questions.explanation: Ethical issues in biology refer to the rightness or wrongness of using biological discoveries and technologies. This encompasses a broad range of concerns including genetic engineering, cloning, stem cell research, animal testing, and environmental impact. Bioethics evaluates the moral implications of biological research and its applications, ensuring that scientific progress aligns with societal values and human rights.

questions.question: Which common characteristics of animals that have balanced arrangement of body parts around a central point or axis?
questions.explanation: Body symmetry is the characteristic of having a balanced arrangement of body parts around a central point (radial symmetry) or central axis (bilateral symmetry). Radial symmetry (like in jellyfish) means body parts are arranged around a central point, while bilateral symmetry (like in humans) means the body can be divided into mirror images along a central plane. Multicellularity, motility, and sensitivity are different animal characteristics.

questions.question: Which one of the following is the common characteristic of invertebrate animals?
options: They possess a backbone and internal skeleton
questions.explanation: Many invertebrate animals, particularly arthropods (insects, crustaceans, arachnids), have an exoskeleton — a hard external covering that provides protection and support for movement. While not all invertebrates have exoskeletons (e.g., worms, jellyfish), it is a common characteristic especially among the most diverse invertebrate group. Invertebrates generally have open circulatory systems, are cold-blooded, and lack a backbone.

questions.question: The form of animal fertilization which young develop within the female by receiving nourishment from mother's blood through placenta is:
questions.explanation: Viviparity is the reproductive mode where the young develop inside the female's body and receive nourishment directly from the mother's blood through a placenta. This is characteristic of most mammals. Oviparity involves laying eggs that develop outside the mother's body, and ovoviviparity involves eggs hatching inside the mother but without placental nourishment.

questions.question: During reproduction, some individual insects develop from unfertilized eggs by the process of:
questions.explanation: Parthenogenesis is a form of asexual reproduction where offspring develop from unfertilized eggs. This occurs naturally in some insect species like aphids and certain bees (where unfertilized eggs develop into male drones). Organogenesis is organ development, metamorphosis is transformation from larva to adult, and gastrulation is an embryonic developmental stage — none involve development from unfertilized eggs.

questions.question: Among which of the following organisms reproduce by external fertilization?
questions.explanation: Frogs reproduce through external fertilization, where the female releases eggs into water and the male releases sperm over them simultaneously. This typically occurs in aquatic environments. Birds, rats, and crocodiles all use internal fertilization, where fertilization occurs inside the female's body. External fertilization is common in aquatic organisms like fish and amphibians.

questions.question: Why temperature is the environmental factor that determine sex during incubation of crocodiles?
questions.explanation: Crocodiles lack sex chromosomes (the typical XX/XY or ZZ/ZW systems found in many other organisms), which is why temperature determines sex during incubation. Without sex chromosomes to genetically determine sex, the incubation temperature triggers different developmental pathways — higher temperatures typically produce females and intermediate temperatures produce males. This is called temperature-dependent sex determination (TSD).

questions.question: Which mating system of birds those involve with two or more members of the opposite sex during a reproductive season?
questions.explanation: Polygamy is the general term for a mating system where an individual has two or more mates of the opposite sex during a reproductive season. It encompasses both polygyny (one male with multiple females) and polyandry (one female with multiple males). Monogamy involves one male and one female pairing. Since the question asks about the general category of having multiple mates, polygamy is the correct answer.

questions.question: Which developmental processes of germ layers later develop into organs and organ system?
questions.explanation: Organogenesis is the developmental process during which the three germ layers (ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm) differentiate and develop into specific organs and organ systems. For example, the ectoderm forms the nervous system and skin, the mesoderm forms muscles and bones, and the endoderm forms the digestive tract lining. Cleavage is the initial cell division, and blastula is an early embryonic stage.

questions.question: All of the following are considered as a beneficial aspects of insects except?
options: Sources of useful product and protein
questions.explanation: Insects are widely edible and serve as important protein sources for humans and animals in many cultures worldwide. The statement that they are 'non-edible type of animals' is incorrect — insects are consumed as food in over 100 countries. The beneficial aspects include pollination (essential for many crops), providing useful products (honey, silk, wax), and serving as biological control agents for pest management.

questions.question: Which one of the following is true about harmful aspect of insect in agriculture?
options: Used as pollinators in crop reproduction
questions.explanation: The harmful aspect of insects in agriculture is damaging crops and reducing production. Insect pests like locusts, aphids, and boll weevils destroy crops by feeding on leaves, stems, fruits, and roots, causing significant economic losses. The other options describe beneficial aspects of insects — pollination, medicine production, and cosmetic/dye production — not harmful ones.

questions.question: Which one of the following examples of behavior describes when an animal stops responding to stimulus or cue after a period of repeated exposure?
questions.explanation: Habituation is a form of learning in which an animal reduces or stops its response to a stimulus after repeated exposure, when the stimulus proves to be irrelevant or harmless. For example, birds initially frightened by scarecrows eventually ignore them. Conditioning involves learning through association, reflex is an automatic response, and imprinting is a critical-period learning that occurs early in life.

questions.question: The Ethiopian wolf lives and hunt in packs. During patrolling their habitat, both male and female wolves of the group maintain their area with urine containing pheromone. This pattern of behavior is an example of:
questions.explanation: This is an example of territorial behavior, where animals mark and defend a specific area against members of the same species. The Ethiopian wolves use urine containing pheromones to define their territory boundaries, signaling to other packs that the area is occupied. Territorial behavior helps reduce competition for resources, ensures breeding space, and protects the pack's hunting grounds.

questions.question: A quick withdrawal of your hand from a hot plate is an example of:
questions.explanation: The quick withdrawal of a hand from a hot plate is a reflex action, which is a type of innate (inborn) behavior. Reflexes are automatic, rapid responses to specific stimuli that do not require conscious thought or learning. The neural pathway (reflex arc) involves sensory neurons, interneurons in the spinal cord, and motor neurons, allowing the response to occur before the brain even registers the pain.

questions.question: An increase in blood sugar level causes the pancreas to release the hormone insulin; insulin lowers blood sugar level, restoring the body to its original blood glucose level by converting glucose to glycogen. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: This is a classic example of negative feedback mechanism. When blood sugar rises above the set point, insulin is released to lower it back to normal. In negative feedback, the response counteracts (reverses) the original change, maintaining homeostasis. Positive feedback would amplify the change (like blood clotting or childbirth contractions), which is not what happens with blood sugar regulation.

questions.question: Which one of the following statement explains the specificity of enzymes?
options: It can be destroyed by excessive heat
It can be used over and over again
Speed up a reaction without being used up
questions.explanation: Enzyme specificity means that each enzyme catalyzes only one specific reaction or acts on only one specific substrate. This is due to the unique three-dimensional shape of the enzyme's active site, which only fits a particular substrate (like a lock and key). The other options describe different enzyme properties: denaturation by heat (A), reusability (B and D), but not specificity.

questions.question: Which protein structures have three-dimensional arrangement and unique shape of all amino acids in a single polypeptide chain?
questions.explanation: Tertiary structure refers to the overall three-dimensional arrangement of all amino acids in a single polypeptide chain. It results from interactions between R-groups (side chains) including hydrogen bonds, disulfide bridges, ionic bonds, and hydrophobic interactions. Primary structure is the amino acid sequence, secondary structure is local folding (alpha helices, beta sheets), and quaternary structure involves multiple polypeptide chains.

questions.question: Which enzyme-substrate model that shows a change in shape of active site of an enzyme?
questions.explanation: The induced-fit model proposes that the enzyme's active site changes shape when the substrate binds, allowing a better fit between enzyme and substrate. This model was proposed by Daniel Koshland as an improvement over the lock and key model, which assumes a rigid active site. The conformational change in the induced-fit model helps catalyze the reaction by placing strain on the substrate bonds.

questions.question: The type of inhibitor that blocks the binding of the substrate to the active site by competition is:
questions.explanation: A competitive inhibitor is a molecule that closely resembles the substrate and competes for binding at the enzyme's active site. By occupying the active site, it prevents the actual substrate from binding. The inhibition can be overcome by increasing substrate concentration, which outcompetes the inhibitor. This is distinct from non-competitive inhibitors that bind elsewhere and change the enzyme's shape.

questions.question: If an enzyme depends on a cofactor for its activity, then the enzyme become active and functional when it is found in the form of:
questions.explanation: A holoenzyme is the complete, active form of an enzyme consisting of the protein component (apoenzyme) plus its cofactor. When the cofactor binds to the apoenzyme (inactive protein), the resulting complex is the holoenzyme, which is catalytically active. The apoenzyme alone is inactive, and coenzymes and mineral ions are types of cofactors, not the active enzyme complex itself.

questions.question: Which of the following is true about feedback inhibition?
options: The end product binds to the active site of enzyme
The end product binds to the allosteric site of enzyme
The shape of the active site is not changed
It cannot prevent enzyme performance activity
questions.explanation: In feedback inhibition, the end product of a metabolic pathway binds to the allosteric site (not the active site) of an enzyme earlier in the pathway. This binding changes the enzyme's shape, reducing its activity and preventing further production of the end product. This is a regulatory mechanism that prevents the overaccumulation of products. The active site shape IS changed as a result, and it DOES prevent enzyme activity.

questions.question: Which commercial fermentation uses of enzyme that is important for clarification of fruit juices?
questions.explanation: Pectinases are enzymes that break down pectin, a structural polysaccharide found in plant cell walls that causes cloudiness in fruit juices. By degrading pectin, pectinases clarify fruit juices, making them clear and visually appealing. This is a major commercial application in the food and beverage industry. Lipases break down fats, amylases break down starch, and proteases break down proteins.

questions.question: Which type organic molecules that are mainly derived from vitamins?
questions.explanation: Coenzymes are organic non-protein molecules derived from vitamins that assist enzymes in catalyzing reactions. Examples include NAD+ (derived from niacin/B3), FAD (derived from riboflavin/B2), and coenzyme A (derived from pantothenic acid/B5). They act as carriers of electrons, atoms, or functional groups between enzymes. Apoenzymes are protein components, cofactors can be inorganic, and holoenzymes are complete enzyme complexes.

questions.question: In an enzyme catalyzed reaction, the substance that is used again and again is:
questions.explanation: Enzymes are catalysts that are not consumed in the reactions they catalyze. After an enzyme catalyzes a reaction and releases the product, it returns to its original form and can catalyze another reaction with a new substrate molecule. This is why a small amount of enzyme can catalyze the conversion of large amounts of substrate — the enzyme is used repeatedly.

questions.question: The process of heating treatment of germinated grain to dry the green malt and prevent from further germination is known as:
questions.explanation: Kilning is the process of heat-treating germinated grain (green malt) to stop further germination, reduce moisture content, and develop flavor compounds. The malt is heated in a kiln at controlled temperatures, which dries the grain and preserves the enzymes needed for the brewing process. Steeping is soaking grain in water, germinating is allowing the grain to sprout, and malting is the overall process.

questions.question: Which one of the following involves cutting back the shoots of plants to about 10-15 cm above the ground and allowing the stumps to regrow and produce more branches?
questions.explanation: Coppicing is a traditional woodland management technique that involves cutting trees or shrubs back to near ground level (typically 10-15 cm above ground) and allowing them to regrow from the stumps. The regrowth produces multiple new shoots, resulting in more branches. This sustainable harvesting method has been used for centuries for producing wood for fuel, fencing, and crafts.

questions.question: The first vitamin produced by the fermentation process of bacteria was ___________.
questions.explanation: Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) was the first vitamin to be commercially produced by bacterial fermentation. The Reichstein process, developed in the 1930s, initially used bacterial fermentation as one step in the chemical synthesis of vitamin C. Modern industrial production of vitamin C uses bacterial fermentation (specifically using strains of Gluconobacter or Ketogulonicigenium) as a key step in the process.

questions.question: The state in which all people have physical, social and economical access to sufficient, safe and nutritious food is:
questions.explanation: Food security exists when all people, at all times, have physical, social, and economic access to sufficient, safe, and nutritious food that meets their dietary needs and food preferences for an active and healthy life. This definition comes from the FAO and encompasses availability, access, utilization, and stability of food supply. Nutrition security goes further to include adequate nutrient absorption.

questions.question: Recently discovered biosensor can detect all except:
questions.explanation: Biosensors are analytical devices that can detect pathogens, herbicides, and toxins, but lactic acid is not typically among the substances that the recently discovered biosensors referenced in the Ethiopian curriculum are designed to detect. While lactic acid biosensors exist in some specialized contexts, the curriculum focuses on biosensors designed for detecting harmful biological and chemical agents.

questions.question: All are correct about genetic fingerprinting except:
options: It is based on RNA analysis
It is unique to individuals except identical twins
It is made by gel electrophoresis in the PCR machine
It is used for crime investigation
questions.explanation: Genetic fingerprinting (DNA fingerprinting) is based on DNA analysis, not RNA analysis. It examines specific regions of DNA (VNTRs and STRs) that vary between individuals. The technique involves extracting DNA, using PCR to amplify specific regions, separating fragments by gel electrophoresis, and creating a unique pattern. It is unique to individuals (except identical twins) and is widely used in forensic science and crime investigation.

questions.question: Which one of the following is the characteristic of bacteria?
options: Their DNA is contained within the cytoplasm of the cell
They are larger than eukaryotic cell
They have cell wall made up of lipoprotein
questions.explanation: Bacteria are prokaryotes, meaning their DNA is located in the cytoplasm in a region called the nucleoid, not enclosed within a nuclear membrane. Bacteria are smaller than eukaryotic cells (not larger), they lack membrane-enclosed organelles (like mitochondria or nucleus), and their cell wall is made of peptidoglycan (not lipoprotein). Option A correctly describes a key prokaryotic characteristic.

questions.question: Which one of the following archaea can live in extremely hot temperature?
questions.explanation: Hyperthermophiles are archaea that thrive in extremely hot temperatures, typically above 80°C, with some surviving above 100°C. They are found in hydrothermal vents, hot springs, and deep-sea vents. Acidophiles prefer acidic environments, psychrophiles live in cold environments (below 15°C), and psychrotrophs can grow in cold but prefer moderate temperatures.

questions.question: Which one of the following fungi has been used as a test organism to determine Mg, Cu, Zn and Mo in the soil?
questions.explanation: Aspergillus niger has been widely used as a test organism in microbiological assays to determine the concentration of trace elements like magnesium (Mg), copper (Cu), zinc (Zn), and molybdenum (Mo) in soil samples. Its growth response is proportional to the available concentration of these minerals, making it a reliable biological indicator for soil mineral analysis.

questions.question: Which one of the following protozoa causes Chagas disease?
questions.explanation: Trypanosoma cruzi causes Chagas disease (American trypanosomiasis), transmitted by triatomine bugs (kissing bugs). Trypanosoma brucei causes African sleeping sickness, Toxoplasma gondii causes toxoplasmosis, and Plasmodium falciparum causes malaria. Chagas disease is endemic in Central and South America and can cause serious cardiac and digestive complications.

questions.question: All are correct about lysogenic cycle except:
options: DNA of the virus integrates into the host DNA
DNA of the virus doesn't integrate into the host DNA
May or may not kill the host cell
questions.explanation: In the lysogenic cycle, the viral DNA integrates into the host cell's genome, becoming a prophage. The statement that 'DNA of the virus doesn't integrate into the host DNA' is incorrect and contradicts the defining feature of the lysogenic cycle. The integrated viral DNA is replicated along with the host DNA during cell division, and the cycle may or may not eventually kill the host cell when it switches to the lytic cycle.

questions.question: Which one is the viral symmetry refers to the shape with 20 triangular faces and 12 corners?
questions.explanation: Icosahedral symmetry refers to a viral capsid shape with 20 triangular faces and 12 corners (vertices). This is the most common capsid shape among animal viruses because it provides an efficient way to enclose the viral genome with minimal protein subunits. Examples include adenoviruses and polioviruses. Helical symmetry produces a rod-shaped capsid, and complex symmetry includes structures that don't fit either category.

questions.question: Which of the following soil bacteria produce powerful protein toxin that used to kill insect pests?
questions.explanation: Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) is a soil bacterium that produces powerful protein toxins (Cry proteins or Bt toxins) that are lethal to certain insect pests, particularly caterpillars and mosquito larvae. These toxins are widely used in biological pest control and have been genetically engineered into crops (Bt cotton, Bt corn) for insect resistance. The other options are fungi, not bacteria.

questions.question: All are correct about catabolism except:
options: It is destructive phase of metabolism
questions.explanation: Catabolism is an energy-releasing (exergonic) process, not an energy-absorbing one. It involves the breakdown of complex molecules into simpler ones, releasing energy in the form of ATP. Options A, B, and C are all correct: catabolism occurs during cellular respiration, it releases energy, and it is the destructive (breakdown) phase of metabolism. Energy-absorbing reactions describe anabolism, not catabolism.

questions.question: Where the light-independent stage of photosynthesis does take place?
questions.explanation: The light-independent stage (Calvin cycle/dark reaction) of photosynthesis takes place in the stroma of the chloroplast. The stroma is the fluid-filled space surrounding the thylakoids and contains the enzymes needed for carbon fixation and sugar synthesis. The light-dependent reactions occur in the thylakoid membranes (grana), while the stroma hosts the Calvin cycle that converts CO2 into glucose.

questions.question: Which one of the following photosynthetic pigments absorbs mostly blue and yellow light?
questions.explanation: Carotenoids are accessory photosynthetic pigments that absorb light primarily in the blue-violet and blue-green regions of the spectrum, and they also absorb some yellow-green light. They appear yellow, orange, or red because they reflect those wavelengths. Carotenoids complement chlorophyll by capturing light energy in wavelength ranges that chlorophyll absorbs less efficiently.

questions.question: Which one of the following photosynthetic pigments found only in the photosynthetic members of Chromista and Dinoflagellates?
questions.explanation: Chlorophyll c is a photosynthetic pigment found exclusively in the Chromista (including diatoms and brown algae) and Dinoflagellates. Unlike chlorophyll a (found in all photosynthetic organisms) and chlorophyll b (found in plants and green algae), chlorophyll c is unique to these specific groups. It serves as an accessory pigment that helps capture light energy in their aquatic environments.

questions.question: All are correct about photosystem II except:
options: Consists of a pair of Chlorophyll 'a' molecules with an absorption peak at 700nm
Consists of a pair of Chlorophyll 'a' molecules with an absorption peak at 680nm
It passes its electrons to photosystem I
It replenishes its lost electrons from photolysis of water
questions.explanation: Photosystem II contains a reaction center with a pair of chlorophyll a molecules that have an absorption peak at 680nm (P680), not 700nm. The 700nm absorption peak (P700) is characteristic of Photosystem I. Options B, C, and D correctly describe PSII: it has P680 chlorophyll, passes electrons to PSI via the electron transport chain, and replenishes its lost electrons through photolysis of water.

questions.question: Which one of the following is incorrect about light independent reaction?
options: It is slower than the light reaction
It is the first step in the mechanism of photosynthesis
It is the second step in the mechanism of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: The light-independent reaction (Calvin cycle) is NOT the first step in photosynthesis — the light-dependent reaction is the first step. The light-dependent reaction captures light energy and produces ATP and NADPH, which are then used in the light-independent reaction. The Calvin cycle is purely enzymatic (option A is correct), it is slower than the light reaction (option B is correct), and it is the second step (option D is correct).

questions.question: Which one of the following processes occurs during light independent reaction of photosynthesis?
options: Change solar energy to chemical energy
questions.explanation: Carbon fixation occurs during the light-independent reaction (Calvin cycle), where CO2 is fixed by combining with ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate (RuBP) using the enzyme RubisCO. The other processes listed all occur during the light-dependent reactions: solar energy conversion to chemical energy (ATP, NADPH), water splitting (photolysis), and NADP+ reduction to NADPH.

questions.question: The first stage of cellular respiration that takes place in the absence of oxygen is called ________.
questions.explanation: Glycolysis is the first stage of cellular respiration and does not require oxygen — it occurs in both aerobic and anaerobic conditions. It takes place in the cytosol and breaks down glucose into two pyruvate molecules, producing 2 ATP and 2 NADH. The Krebs cycle, electron transport chain, and link reaction all require oxygen and occur only during aerobic respiration.

questions.question: The amount of net ATP that glycolysis provides to a cell is:
questions.explanation: Glycolysis produces a net gain of 2 ATP per glucose molecule. Although 4 ATP are produced during glycolysis, 2 ATP are consumed in the initial investment phase (phosphorylation of glucose and fructose-6-phosphate), resulting in a net gain of 2 ATP. The 38 ATP figure represents the total from complete aerobic respiration, and 36 is an alternative estimate for total respiration.

questions.question: In the mitochondrial matrix pyruvic acid reacts with a molecule of coenzyme A to form:
questions.explanation: In the mitochondrial matrix, pyruvic acid (pyruvate) undergoes oxidative decarboxylation, reacting with coenzyme A to form acetyl CoA. This link reaction also produces one CO2 and one NADH per pyruvate molecule. Acetyl CoA then enters the Krebs cycle. Ethyl alcohol and lactic acid are products of fermentation (not aerobic respiration), and NAD is a coenzyme, not a product of this reaction.

questions.question: A biochemical pathway that breaks down Acetyl CoA is ___________.
questions.explanation: The Krebs cycle (citric acid cycle) is the biochemical pathway that breaks down acetyl CoA. Acetyl CoA enters the cycle by combining with oxaloacetate to form citrate, which then undergoes a series of reactions producing CO2, NADH, FADH2, and ATP. The other options describe different stages: glycolysis breaks down glucose, pyruvate oxidation converts pyruvate to acetyl CoA, and the link reaction is another name for pyruvate oxidation.

questions.question: The final electron acceptor in the electron transport chain is ___________.
questions.explanation: Oxygen is the final electron acceptor in the electron transport chain. At the end of the chain, electrons are transferred to oxygen, which then combines with hydrogen ions (protons) to form water. This is why oxygen is essential for aerobic respiration — without it, the electron transport chain backs up and ATP production is severely limited.

questions.question: The electron transport chain and the enzyme ATP synthase are embedded in the:
questions.explanation: The electron transport chain and ATP synthase are embedded in the cristae — the folds of the inner mitochondrial membrane. These folds increase the surface area for the electron transport chain proteins and ATP synthase, maximizing ATP production. The thylakoid membrane and grana are found in chloroplasts (for photosynthesis), and the stroma is the fluid inside chloroplasts.

questions.question: All of the following occurs during Lactic acid fermentation except?
options: Lactic acid accumulates in muscle cells
Liver converts Lactic acid to pyruvic acid
questions.explanation: Ethyl alcohol is NOT produced during lactic acid fermentation — it is produced during alcoholic fermentation (in yeast). Lactic acid fermentation converts pyruvate to lactic acid, regenerating NAD+ from NADH (so NADH is oxidized). Lactic acid accumulates in muscle cells during intense exercise, and the liver later converts it back to pyruvic acid (Cori cycle). The release of ethyl alcohol is exclusive to alcoholic fermentation.
